title: Replacing a worn radiator compression valve
subtitle: Swap a weeping or seized lockshield or wheelhead valve on a radiator
excerpt: How to replace a leaking or seized radiator valve: draining the affected radiator, removing the old valve, fitting the new valve on the pipe with a fresh olive, and refilling.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pipe joint that uses a brass olive compressed between two mating surfaces as the pipe nut is tightened, creating a watertight seal without soldering. The olive deforms permanently onto the pipe as the nut is tightened — once compressed, the olive and nut remain on the pipe; a new olive is fitted if the joint is re-made.

The valve on the flow or return side of a radiator that is set once during system commissioning to balance flow across the circuit. It is covered by a plastic cap and not adjusted during normal use. Distinct from the wheelhead valve (or TRV head) on the other end, which is the user-controlled heat control.

content.text: A radiator valve that weeps around the gland or through a
joint needs replacing rather than re-tightening; further tightening splits the olive or damages the valve body. Either the
or the wheelhead/TRV valve can be replaced this way; the procedure is the same for both ends.
This job requires draining the radiator. Have towels and a container ready for the residual water in the radiator body. The water in a central heating system is often dark and will stain surfaces.
Step 1, isolate and drain the radiator

Close both valves on the radiator fully: the thermostatic or wheelhead valve on one side, and the lockshield valve on the other (note how many turns the lockshield takes to close so you can restore its setting later). Attach a hose to the bleed valve at the top of the radiator and run it to a bucket. Open the bleed valve with a radiator bleed key to break the vacuum, then undo the union nut on the valve being replaced to drain water from that end.

Undo the valve tail nut at the radiator end first, then the pipe compression nut at the pipe end. Support the pipe with one hand to prevent it dropping or bending as the nut releases. Remove the old olive from the pipe; if it is compressed hard onto the pipe, it may need to be slit with a knife and prised off.
Step 3, prepare and fit the new valve

Clean the pipe end with the emery cloth until it is bright copper. Slide the new compression nut and a new olive onto the pipe. Screw the valve tail into the radiator union hand-tight first, then tighten with the adjustable spanner while counter-holding with the stilson wrench. Offer the valve body to the pipe and tighten the compression nut: finger-tight plus a half turn for a new olive.

Close the bleed valve. Open the new valve to its operating position. Re-open the lockshield valve to its previous setting. Check the system pressure gauge at the boiler and top up via the filling loop if needed. Open the bleed valve briefly to release trapped air until water runs out, then close. Run the heating and check both ends of the new valve for drips.

attrs.cause: The olive has not been compressed fully, or the pipe end is rough or not square.
attrs.fix: Drain, cut back the pipe to expose fresh copper, fit a new olive, and re-make the joint.

attrs.symptom: Valve tail will not seat in the radiator union
attrs.cause: The tail thread is a different standard (BSP vs metric) from the union.
attrs.fix: Match the tail thread to the radiator union. Most UK radiators use 1/2 inch BSP; check the union marking.
